nativeRange.habitat: Coastal lagoons, brackish marshes, springs, first-order streams, irrigation canals, and small lakes; marginal zones with dense submerged or filamentous vegetation in fresh to slightly brackish water

decorPreferences: dense filamentous algae or java moss as spawning substrate
floating or fine-leaved plants for cover
rocks or driftwood to break sightlines and reduce male aggression

diet.notes: Omnivorous with a marked vegetarian tendency. Staple diet of algae-based flake, spirulina wafers, and blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach, cucumber). Supplement regularly with small live or frozen foods — Artemia nauplii, Daphnia, Mysis, and bloodworm — especially during the spring and summer breeding season. Wild fish feed on insect larvae, crustaceans, and algae.

goodWith: other hard-water toothcarps in spacious biotope setups (with caution)
robust hard-alkaline livebearers in sufficiently large tanks
avoidWith: community fish — males fight conspecifics to the death
small or delicate fish that will be harassed

imageCandidates.notes: Male specimen; standard FishBase reference photo. Verify availability before uploading to R2.

summary: A jewel-like euryhaline killifish from Turkey and the Levant, the Iridescent Toothcarp is noted for the breeding male’s iridescent blue-white spots on a near-black body. Requires hard alkaline water and a species-only or single-genus setup due to extreme male aggression. IUCN Endangered; a conservation-interest species.
careNotes:
The Iridescent Toothcarp (Paraphanius mento) is a small but strikingly patterned Old World killifish from the family Aphaniidae — a lineage separate from the familiar New World Cyprinodontidae. The genus Paraphanius was erected by Freyhof et al. (2017) based on molecular and morphological revision of the old broad Aphanius genus; hobbyist literature and older trade labels frequently still use Aphanius mento.

This species tolerates an unusually wide temperature band (approximately 10–28°C) reflecting its origin in a continental climate where Turkish and Levantine springs can cool dramatically in winter. A cooler period (12–16°C) over winter is recommended to condition fish for spring spawning; no heater is required in temperate fish rooms. Hard, alkaline water (pH 7.5–9.0, gH 10–30) is essential — the species does not tolerate soft or acidic conditions. Most populations benefit from the addition of 1–3 g/L marine salt, though purely freshwater populations are known; the species is genuinely euryhaline, naturally occurring in both freshwater springs and slightly brackish coastal lagoons and marshes.

Male aggression is the central husbandry challenge. Males of P. mento are regarded as the most aggressive within the genus and will fight rival males to the death. A species-only or single-genus setup is strongly recommended. The standard approach is one male with two or three females in a 60 cm tank for a pair/trio, or a 120 cm+ tank for a small harem group. Heavy planting and visual barriers (rocks, driftwood) are mandatory to give females refuge from persistent male pursuit. Females may also be killed by an over-attentive male in a bare or sparsely decorated tank.

Diet is omnivorous with a strong vegetable bias. Spirulina-based flakes, algae wafers, and blanched vegetables form the staple, supplemented with live or frozen Artemia, Daphnia, and Mysis shrimp, particularly in the breeding season. The species is classified Endangered by the IUCN; wild populations face ongoing habitat loss from agricultural water abstraction, pollution, and habitat degradation across its fragmented Turkish/Levantine range. Hobbyist breeding programmes contribute meaningfully to ex-situ conservation.
breedingNotes: A fractional (continuous) spawner during the warmer months, typically April through September. Eggs are deposited individually onto filamentous algae, java moss, or acrylic wool mops, to which they attach by adhesive filaments. Adults will eat eggs; it is best practice to remove the spawning medium daily and incubate eggs separately in a small container with methylene blue at the same temperature. Incubation takes 6–14 days depending on temperature. Fry are free-swimming immediately and accept Artemia nauplii or micro-worm from the first day. Sexual maturity is reached in approximately 12–16 weeks. A gradual temperature rise into the low 20s°C after a cool winter period is the most reliable spawning trigger. Productivity can be very high — experienced keepers report trios yielding hundreds of eggs per week at peak season.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Paraphanius mento (Aphanius mento)
primary.notes: Primary care source. Provided water parameters (temp 2–30°C, pH 7.5–9.0, hardness 179–536 ppm), adult size (50–70 mm), minimum tank dimensions, diet, aggression notes, breeding season and incubation details, and native range. Salinity noted as 1–3 g/L marine salt beneficial for most populations.

additional.name: FishBase — Paraphanius mento
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Aphaniidae, Cyprinodontiformes), native countries (Iran, Iraq, Syria, Jordan, Israel, Lebanon, Turkey), max size (5 cm TL males), temperature (10–25°C subtropical), diet (insect larvae, crustaceans, algae), and habitat (shallow vegetated water, benthopelagic). IUCN LC on FishBase refers to older 2013 assessment; task brief and IUCN species PDF 107411865 indicate Endangered at population level.

additional.name: British Killifish Association — Paraphanius mento
additional.notes: Hobbyist care notes confirming alkaline water preference, temperature range (65–75°F / ~18–24°C breeding window), male aggression, egg hatching at 7–10 days (72°F), lifespan up to 3 years, and high fry productivity (300+ eggs/week from a good trio at peak).

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Aphanius mento (species PDF 107411865)
additional.notes: Conservation status: Endangered. Range fragmented on Turkish/Syrian border; continuing decline from agricultural water abstraction, habitat degradation, and direct persecution. Fewer than 10 known locations in Turkey. Corroborates task brief Endangered designation despite the older 2013 FishBase-linked LC assessment.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Iridescent toothcarp
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Aphaniidae), range (Iraq, Israel, Jordan, Lebanon, Palestine, Syria, Turkey), adult size (5 cm TL), type locality (Mosul, Iraq, Heckel 1843), and genus revision context (Paraphanius).

fish.breedingNotes: Fractional spawner April–September on algae/mops; incubation 6–14 days. Remove eggs daily to avoid predation. Fry accept Artemia nauplii from day one. A cool winter rest of 12–16°C followed by gradual warming triggers spawning.
